startScreenOptions.title: PY9.1-PY9.9 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 7 embedded questions for PY9.1-PY9.9.

interactions.label: In a 46,XY individual with complete androgen insensitivity syndrome, which of th

params.question: In a 46,XY individual with complete androgen insensitivity syndrome, which of the following best explains the external female phenotype?

answers.text: The SRY gene is absent from the Y chromosome

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: In AIS, the SRY gene is present and testes do form — the defect is in the androgen receptor, not gonadal determination.

answers.text: Without testosterone, the gonads default to forming ovaries instead of testes

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: In a 46,XY individual, SRY drives testis formation regardless of androgen signalling. The gonads are testes — it is the peripheral tissues that fail to virilise.

answers.text: Tissues cannot respond to testosterone despite its production by the testes

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: In complete AIS, testes produce testosterone normally, but a non-functional androgen receptor means target tissues cannot respond. External genitalia therefore develop along the default female pathway.

answers.text: Anti-Müllerian hormone causes regression of the Wolffian ducts

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: AMH causes regression of the Müllerian ducts (uterus, fallopian tubes), not the Wolffian ducts. In AIS, Müllerian regression occurs normally because AMH receptors are unaffected.

overallFeedback.feedback: In AIS, testes form and produce both testosterone and AMH. However, a non-functional androgen receptor means tissues cannot respond to testosterone, resulting in female external genitalia despite a 46,XY karyotype.
Well done. AIS demonstrates that virilisation requires both testosterone production and a functional androgen receptor — the tissue response, not the hormone alone, determines phenotype.

interactions.label: The pampiniform plexus of veins surrounding the testicular artery functions prim

params.question: The pampiniform plexus of veins surrounding the testicular artery functions primarily as:

answers.text: A countercurrent heat exchanger that cools arterial blood entering the testis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pampiniform plexus transfers heat from warm arterial blood to cooler venous blood returning from the testis, maintaining the 2–4°C temperature difference below core body temperature that spermatogenesis requires.

answers.text: A reservoir for storing testosterone before systemic release

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Testosterone is a steroid hormone carried in the bloodstream bound to sex hormone-binding globulin, not stored in venous plexuses. Its regulation depends on synthesis rate in Leydig cells.

answers.text: A venous drainage pathway that bypasses the inferior vena cava

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pampiniform plexus drains into the testicular vein, which empties into the renal vein (left) or IVC (right). Its primary functional significance is thermoregulation, not alternative drainage.

answers.text: A pressure-regulating mechanism that prevents testicular torsion

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Testicular torsion involves twisting of the spermatic cord and is a structural issue related to the tunica vaginalis, not venous plexus function.

overallFeedback.feedback: The pampiniform plexus acts as a countercurrent heat exchanger — cool venous blood absorbs heat from warm arterial blood, keeping testicular temperature 2–4°C below core body temperature, essential for spermatogenesis.
Excellent. This countercurrent mechanism is why the scrotum sits outside the body — and why varicocele (dilated testicular veins) raises testicular temperature and impairs spermatogenesis.

interactions.label: Inhibin produced by Sertoli cells selectively suppresses both FSH and LH secreti

params.question: Inhibin produced by Sertoli cells selectively suppresses both FSH and LH secretion from the anterior pituitary.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Inhibin selectively suppresses FSH only, not both gonadotrophins. Testosterone, not inhibin, feeds back to reduce both GnRH and LH. This distinction allows the HPG axis to separately fine-tune spermatogenesis and androgen production.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Inhibin selectively suppresses only FSH, not both gonadotrophins. Testosterone feeds back to suppress both GnRH and LH. This selective mechanism allows independent regulation of spermatogenesis (FSH-driven) and testosterone production (LH-driven).

interactions.label: In a normal 28-day menstrual cycle, what triggers the rupture of the Graafian fo

params.question: In a normal 28-day menstrual cycle, what triggers the rupture of the Graafian follicle at ovulation?

answers.text: A sharp decline in progesterone from the corpus luteum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The corpus luteum has not yet formed at ovulation — it develops only after the follicle ruptures. Progesterone levels are low during the follicular phase.

answers.text: A surge in luteinising hormone triggered by rising oestrogen levels

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Rising oestrogen from the dominant Graafian follicle triggers a positive feedback surge in LH from the anterior pituitary around day 14. This LH surge causes the follicle wall to rupture, releasing the oocyte.

answers.text: A sudden increase in follicle-stimulating hormone during the luteal phase

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: FSH is highest during the early follicular phase and declines before ovulation. It is the LH surge, not an FSH rise, that triggers ovulation.

answers.text: Mechanical pressure from adjacent growing follicles in the ovary

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Ovulation is a hormonally driven process. While multiple follicles grow initially, only the dominant follicle ovulates — triggered by the LH surge, not mechanical forces.

overallFeedback.feedback: Ovulation is triggered by a surge in LH around day 14. Rising oestrogen from the dominant Graafian follicle switches from negative to positive feedback on the pituitary, causing the LH surge that ruptures the follicle.

Well done. The mid-cycle LH surge is the key ovulatory trigger — understanding this positive feedback switch is essential for both reproductive physiology and the rationale behind hormonal contraception.

metadata.title: In a normal 28-day menstrual cycle, what

interactions.label: Human placental lactogen (hPL) has an anti-insulin effect during pregnancy. Whic

params.question: Human placental lactogen (hPL) has an anti-insulin effect during pregnancy. Which of the following is the physiological purpose of this action?

answers.text: To prevent maternal weight gain during the third trimester

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: hPL promotes maternal lipolysis and insulin resistance, which does not prevent weight gain. Its role is to ensure adequate glucose reaches the fetus, not to regulate maternal body weight.

answers.text: To stimulate maternal pancreatic beta cells to produce more insulin

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While the pancreas does increase insulin output as a compensatory response, this is a secondary effect — not the physiological purpose of hPL, which is glucose redirection to the fetus.

answers.text: To protect the placenta from glucose-mediated oxidative damage

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The anti-insulin effect of hPL redirects glucose toward the fetus, not away from the placenta. Placental protection from oxidative stress involves different mechanisms entirely.

answers.text: To divert glucose from the mother to the growing fetus

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: hPL creates insulin resistance in maternal tissues, forcing the mother to rely more on fatty acids for energy. This ensures glucose is preferentially available for the fetus. When maternal compensation fails, gestational diabetes develops.

overallFeedback.feedback: hPL induces insulin resistance in maternal tissues so that glucose is preferentially diverted to the growing fetus. This mechanism explains why gestational diabetes develops when the maternal pancreas cannot compensate adequately.
Excellent. hPL's anti-insulin effect is a fetal survival strategy — it ensures glucose supply to the growing baby, but can tip into gestational diabetes when maternal compensation is overwhelmed.

interactions.label: A woman with clinical features of molar pregnancy tests negative on a home urine

params.question: A woman with clinical features of molar pregnancy tests negative on a home urine pregnancy strip. Which phenomenon best explains this false-negative result?

answers.text: Molar pregnancies do not produce hCG because trophoblastic tissue is absent

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Molar pregnancies involve abnormal trophoblastic proliferation, which produces disproportionately high levels of hCG — the opposite of absent production.

answers.text: Extremely high hCG concentrations saturate the test antibodies, producing a false negative

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is the hook effect. When hCG is extremely high, as in gestational trophoblastic disease, both capture and detection antibodies become saturated and the test paradoxically reads negative. A quantitative blood test or diluted urine sample confirms the diagnosis.

answers.text: Home urine strips detect only the alpha subunit of hCG, which moles do not secrete

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Home pregnancy strips detect the beta subunit of intact hCG. Molar tissue produces abundant hCG with both subunits, making this explanation incorrect.

answers.text: The detection threshold of home strips is too high for the hCG levels seen in molar pregnancy

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Home strips detect hCG at 20–25 mIU/mL, and molar pregnancies produce levels far exceeding any threshold. The issue is antibody saturation at extremely high concentrations, not an insensitive test.

overallFeedback.feedback: The hook effect occurs when extremely high hCG saturates both capture and detection antibodies in the test strip, producing a false-negative result. Always confirm with a quantitative blood test or diluted urine when clinical suspicion is high.
Well done. The hook effect is a vital clinical pitfall — always suspect it when a home test is negative but clinical features suggest molar pregnancy or choriocarcinoma.

metadata.title: A woman with clinical features of molar

interactions.label: A woman being evaluated for infertility has infrequent, irregular periods. Ultra

params.question: A woman being evaluated for infertility has infrequent, irregular periods. Ultrasound shows multiple small peripheral follicles in both ovaries. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her infertility?

answers.text: Asherman syndrome with intrauterine adhesions

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Asherman syndrome causes scanty or absent periods from intrauterine adhesions, usually after surgery or infection. Ultrasound would show a thin endometrium, not multiple ovarian follicles.

answers.text: Endometriosis causing bilateral tubal blockage

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tubal blockage from endometriosis impairs egg transport but does not cause anovulation. The finding of multiple peripheral follicles with irregular cycles points to an ovulatory disorder, not a tubal problem.

answers.text: Polycystic ovary syndrome causing anovulation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Irregular, infrequent periods with multiple small peripheral ovarian follicles on ultrasound is the classic presentation of PCOS — the leading cause of ovulatory dysfunction, accounting for 25–30% of female infertility.

answers.text: Premature ovarian insufficiency with follicular depletion

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Premature ovarian insufficiency involves depletion of follicles, so ultrasound would show few or no follicles, not multiple small ones. FSH would also be markedly elevated.

overallFeedback.feedback: The combination of irregular periods and multiple small peripheral ovarian follicles on ultrasound is the hallmark of PCOS, the most common cause of ovulatory dysfunction and anovulatory female infertility.

Excellent. Recognising the PCOS pattern — oligo-ovulation with polycystic ovarian morphology — is essential, as it is the single most common cause of anovulatory infertility in women of reproductive age.
